title: Building a Grow-Only Counter on a Sequentially Consistent KV Store
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-20, Reading time: 14 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fbrunocalza.me%2Fblog%2F2026%2F04%2F13%2Fbuilding-a-grow-only-counter-on-a-sequentially-consistent-kv-store.html%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019daa5d12a4-a8eff751-0bfa-48ba-a070-4de1c73d542d-000000/EETXa5rqgJXMvFQg2NYVrZHdKGUEXoRzqXCNFtEpyG8=452
summary: Building a Grow-Only Counter on a Sequentially Consistent KV Store (14 minute read) A grow-only counter on a sequentially consistent key-value store can fail because stale reads and concurrent updates may lose increments, even when using compare-and-swap. The article explains CRDT-style counters, consistency models, and why a linearizable store or gossip-based design gives more reliable results.

title: PostgreSQL MVCC, Byte by Byte
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-20, Reading time: 9 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fboringsql.com%2Fposts%2Fpostgresql-mvcc-byte-by-byte%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019daa5d12a4-a8eff751-0bfa-48ba-a070-4de1c73d542d-000000/V0jZ4uTXXPfx3nTelGNmNp-gmYMkDThsYIvRXG8Gi7g=452
summary: PostgreSQL MVCC, Byte by Byte (9 minute read) PostgreSQL's MVCC works by storing multiple versions of each row on disk. Every tuple contains xmin and xmax fields that determine its visibility to transactions. Readers never block writers, and writers never block readers. When a row is updated or deleted, a new version is created instead of overwriting the old one. Old versions are cleaned up later by the vacuum process.

title: Data Shift vs. Data Drift
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-20, Reading time: 6 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.montecarlodata.com%2Fblog-data-shift-vs-data-drift%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019daa5d12a4-a8eff751-0bfa-48ba-a070-4de1c73d542d-000000/OGFxF8g85gTnhRrHxI4qC8V2y1FXIDzgx-37aVF3_WA=452
summary: Data Shift vs. Data Drift (6 minute read) Data drift refers to gradual changes in the statistical properties of data (e.g., mean, distribution), while data shift is a sudden, more severe change often caused by upstream system changes, schema updates, or business events. Correctly distinguishing between the two is critical because they require different alerting strategies.

title: There Is No Pivot
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-20, Reading time: 9 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fbenn.substack.com%2Fp%2Fthere-is-no-pivot%3Futm_source=tldrfounders/1/0100019daacb6f5f-8748b1ff-3915-4824-80c7-6825f531dc55-000000/9_vNB5NhLL_wrgUXzbCIPjVaBYoThLVMcWHD3SLulzk=452
summary: There Is No Pivot (9 minute read) When software was expensive to build, commitment was cheap, and changing direction was the crisis that demanded a name. Now that building is cheap, the economics invert. Commitment becomes the expensive thing, and iteration is the default mode. The companies winning now look less like ships changing course and more like test kitchens running permanent R&D, which is why calling any given change a pivot has started to sound like a word from a different era.

summary: Recent Aave rsETH Exploit can be eliminated by a new n-VM architecture (3 minute read) The n-VM architecture integrates heterogeneous virtual machines like EVM and SVM into a single consensus and shared state tree. summary: Chronicle – Codex (6 minute read) Chronicle, available for ChatGPT Pro users on macOS, augments Codex by using screen context for memory building, helping Codex understand ongoing work with less context restatement. Be aware of prompt injection risks from screen content, and pause Chronicle during sensitive work to prevent unwanted context capture.

title: Even 'uncensored' models can't say what they want
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-21, Reading time: 6 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fmorgin.ai%2Farticles%2Feven-uncensored-models-cant-say-what-they-want.html%3Futm_source=tldrai/1/0100019db032e658-6e5cacae-6b6a-4fcf-83b9-846d8001a2e8-000000/ovNIvpvDrdSjucWJ15wAtxb6O7H_Xub5WeK-PwyHKjI=452
summary: Even 'uncensored' models can't say what they want (6 minute read) Even uncensored models quietly nudge language away from the words that sentences actually want. summary: Announcing TypeScript 7.0 Beta (16 minute read) TypeScript 7.0 Beta is built on a completely new foundation, making it about 10 times faster than TypeScript 6.0. title: Agents with Taste
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-22, Reading time: 6 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Femilkowal.ski%2Fui%2Fagents-with-taste%3Futm_source=tldrnewsletter/1/0100019db4b76202-3e4a636d-cd76-4b0d-a4e2-237b645fd95f-000000/hXPbOpPzM8y7L88U3xEMjHRDmAispk4q5EMWeI5ipPE=452
summary: Agents with Taste (6 minute read) Coding agents don't quite know what great feels like when it comes to visual work like animations. One way to get around this is to create a skill file for each aspect of an interface. Describe the rules of what 'great' feels like in that context and give that to agents so they can follow them. This results in AI that has the taste and knowledge to produce significantly better results.

title: Building a fault-tolerant metrics storage system at Airbnb
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-22, Reading time: 9 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinks.tldrnewsletter.com%2Fy06Q7P/1/0100019db4e0f39a-69c3b02e-233e-4af2-893a-0ffcaa537a6c-000000/kvoO87IxTFRhdAyc2jZaBvSVaHSsiVllVSqzDgw-e3U=452
summary: Building a fault-tolerant metrics storage system at Airbnb (9 minute read) To manage 1.3 billion active time series at such a large scale, Airbnb developed a fault-tolerant metrics storage system using shuffle sharding and a multi-cluster architecture for improved resiliency and isolation. This automated solution successfully stabilized data operations and streamlined deployments by treating clusters as disposable components, despite the added complexity of federated querying.

title: Inside GitHub's Fake Star Economy
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-22, Reading time: 15 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fawesomeagents.ai%2Fnews%2Fgithub-fake-stars-investigation%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019db4e0f39a-69c3b02e-233e-4af2-893a-0ffcaa537a6c-000000/D8fYz6Cibc0d2mJluRk6gDyaqExpGrkX_nXJrjv88d0=452
summary: Inside GitHub's Fake Star Economy (15 minute read) A widespread fake star economy on GitHub involves startups purchasing millions of fake stars to artificially inflate repository popularity and attract venture capital funding, which relies on star counts as a key signal. This persistent manipulation can be detected by analyzing metrics like low fork-to-star ratios. Combating the deception requires investors to adopt more robust metrics and for GitHub to implement structural changes.

As he transitions to Executive Chairman, Cook leaves a legacy of large market value. His successor must address the long-term strategic challenges in AI and supply chain sustainability.

comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-22, Reading time: 4 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fsawyer.dev%2Fposts%2Fpractitioner-program-analysis%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019db4e0f39a-69c3b02e-233e-4af2-893a-0ffcaa537a6c-000000/oyB7GyWgENwHfHT6ZPYxi3L9XY_XHw6Amd2Orv87cr0=452
summary: My practitioner view of program analysis (4 minute read) "Correct" software is code aligned with the collective intent and understanding of all stakeholders, necessitating a bridge over the semantic gap where code fails to communicate its underlying concepts. title: Bitcoin and Quantum Computing: A Roadmap
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-22, Reading time: 8 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fnehanarula.org%2F2026%2F04%2F20%2Fbitcoin-and-quantum-a-roadmap.html%3Futm_source=tldrcrypto/1/0100019db515482d-18b49ab7-666f-4654-a069-5fe9d8012cdf-000000/hl4_AldeMGcrH7OMafqm0QL0nKoWnhMYZp3FedfMaTw=452
summary: Bitcoin and Quantum Computing: A Roadmap (8 minute read) This post proposes a pragmatic roadmap to secure Bitcoin against Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computers. This incremental approach prioritizes immediate, low-risk mitigations while deferring complex, high-stakes decisions regarding legacy coin security.

summary: Great Companies Are Built in Hackathons (8 minute read) PostHog has shipped four real products out of hackathons. Logs became one after someone ran the math on Datadog's bill and saw $260K a month. The rules are what make it work. Nothing from the roadmap, no sneaking regular work in, non-engineers participate. It treats hackathons the way most companies treat roadmap planning, which is why they actually produce companies.

title: The Rumpelstiltskin Effect: Why We Buy
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-22, Reading time: 3 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fckarchive.com%2Fb%2Fp9ueh9h2oorv8im6ggw6kapqemm24brhkz6v6%3Futm_source=tldrfounders/1/0100019db518fb33-f1ab7625-3b74-4b5b-baa6-ff23ce26c50c-000000/mEKa90nmCACR0ciof5QQIkmeFJu0LkxWEF1g-3BE7qU=452
summary: The Rumpelstiltskin Effect: Why We Buy (3 minute read) Naming a problem gives you power over it. People feel better the moment a vague struggle gets a clean label, even before anything's actually fixed. Febreze coined "noseblind" and suddenly everyone was panic-sniffing their couch cushions. Snickers named the feeling "hangry." A consultant pitching generic marketing help loses, but the same consultant selling a "Content Paralysis Crusher" package closes.
